vulnerabilities.title: emergencyWithdraw Unchecked Underflow Enables Complete Fund Drain
vulnerabilities.summary: emergencyWithdraw calls _processWithdrawal which uses unchecked arithmetic to subtract from balances[user][token] and totalDeposits[token] without any balance validation, zero-amount check, or access restriction. Any user with zero balance can call emergencyWithdraw(token, 1) when the contract holds tokens from other depositors. The unchecked subtraction wraps balances[attacker][token] to type(uint128).max - 0 (approximately 3.4e38). The safeTransfer succeeds because the contract holds tokens. T

description.desc: unchecked { balances[user][token] -= amount; totalDeposits[token] -= amount; } with no balance check in emergencyWithdraw or _processWithdrawal

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. Complete loss of ALL deposited tokens for every token type in the contract. Any address can exploit this with zero capital. No admin/pause mechanism exists to prevent or mitigate the attack. All depositors lose all funds with no recovery possibility. The vulnerability is trivially exploitable and can be automated to drain all token types in rapid succession.

vulnerabilities.title: cancel() Missing Authorization Allows Anyone to Cancel Any Order
vulnerabilities.summary: The cancel() function checks only that order.maker != address(0) (order exists) but never verifies msg.sender == order.maker. The Unauthorized error is defined but never used. Any external caller can cancel any active order. While funds are refunded to the original maker, this enables systematic orderbook destruction: an attacker can cancel every order in the DEX, disrupting all trading activity at near-zero cost.

description.desc: cancel() checks order.maker != address(0) but never checks msg.sender == order.maker; error Unauthorized() defined but unused

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. While no direct fund theft occurs (refunds go to makers), the impact is: (1) Complete denial of service — entire orderbook can be wiped at minimal cost. (2) Front-running fills — orders can be cancelled before fills execute. (3) Market maker griefing — makes providing liquidity impossible. (4) DEX becomes permanently unusable if attacker runs a bot. The defined but unused `Unauthorized` error strongly indicates this was an oversight, not intentional design.

vulnerabilities.title: MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION Constant Defined but Never Enforced in placeOrder
vulnerabilities.summary: The constant MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION = 1e16 (1% from parity) is defined to restrict stablecoin order prices near 1:1, but placeOrder() never references it. Any non-zero price is accepted, including extreme values far from parity. This enables deceptive orders at prices that exploit automated taker bots or confused users, and is a prerequisite for the uint128 truncation scenario in fillOrder.

description.desc: MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION defined at line 46-47 as uint128 public constant = 1e16; placeOrder validates price != 0 at line 189 but never checks |price - 1e18| <= MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity as a standalone issue (SWC-135 dead code / missing validation). The direct impact is orderbook pollution with extreme-priced orders that violate the stablecoin DEX's core design premise of near-parity trading. However, the severity escalates when combined with the uint128 truncation in fillOrder (INV-STATE-001), as MAX_PRICE_DEVIATION enforcement would have prevented the extreme prices needed to trigger quoteAmount overflow. The missing check defeats a deliberate safety mechanism the developers defined.

vulnerabilities.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Deposit Creates Accounting Deficit Enabling Last-Withdrawer Loss
vulnerabilities.summary: The deposit() function credits balances[msg.sender][token] += amount without measuring the actual tokens received by the contract. For fee-on-transfer tokens, the contract receives less than amount but credits the full amount. Over multiple deposits, the cumulative accounting deficit grows. Later withdrawers find the contract insolvent—actual balance < total recorded balances—and cannot withdraw their funds.

description.desc: deposit() credits balances[msg.sender][token] += amount after safeTransferFrom without measuring actual received via balanceOf delta

vulnerabilities.impact: For any fee-on-transfer token deposited into the contract, the accounting invariant (contract balance >= sum of all user balances) is violated. The deficit grows linearly with deposits. Eventually, the contract becomes insolvent for that token, and late withdrawers lose funds proportional to the cumulative transfer fee deficit. This is a direct violation of INV-ECON-001 (physical solvency) and INV-ECON-002 (conservation).

vulnerabilities.title: Incomplete Order Struct Deletion in _cancelOrder Leaves Stale Storage Data
vulnerabilities.summary: _cancelOrder deletes maker, baseToken, quoteToken, price, and amount from the order struct but does NOT delete remaining, prev, and next. Conversely, _removeFilledOrder clears maker, prev, and next but does NOT clear baseToken, quoteToken, price, amount, or remaining. Both cleanup paths leave partial stale data. While orders[orderId].maker == address(0) gates direct re-interaction, stale prev/next pointers and remaining values persist in storage, which could confuse off-chain indexers or future

description.desc: _cancelOrder individually deletes maker/baseToken/quoteToken/price/amount but NOT remaining/prev/next; _removeFilledOrder zeros maker/prev/next but NOT baseToken/quoteToken/price/amount/remaining

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The code contains a genuine defect - incomplete struct cleanup in two separate functions, each missing different fields. While the maker==address(0) check prevents direct re-exploitation within the current contract, the stale data is observable through getOrder(), wastes gas, and creates an inconsistent storage state. Off-chain indexers or future contract integrations could misinterpret the stale data. The defect also forfeits gas refunds that would be obtained from properly zeroing all storage slots.

vulnerabilities.title: Orderbook Cluttering and Capital Lockup via Dust Partial Fills
vulnerabilities.summary: `placeOrder` enforces a `MIN_ORDER_SIZE` to prevent dust, but `fillOrder` allows partial fills that can leave `order.remaining` below this minimum. An attacker can partially fill orders leaving exactly 1 wei remaining. These dust orders remain in the linked list, cluttering the orderbook and locking the maker's remaining capital until they spend gas to manually cancel.

description.desc: Partial fill allows order.remaining to fall below MIN_ORDER_SIZE

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The attack is a griefing/DoS vector rather than a direct fund theft. Impact includes: (1) Orderbook pollution with dust orders that inflate linked list traversal costs, (2) Maker capital lockup of dust amounts recoverable only through gas-expensive cancel() calls, (3) Degraded UX for market makers who must actively monitor and clean up dust orders, (4) Increased gas costs for getOrders() view calls as the linked list grows with uncleanable dust entries. The attack cost is low (standard fill gas + actual trade at market price), making it economically viable for griefing.

vulnerabilities.title: Cost-Free Wash Trading for Volume Manipulation
vulnerabilities.summary: The protocol does not charge trading fees and does not prevent a user from filling their own orders (`msg.sender == order.maker`). A user can place an order and immediately fill it themselves to generate infinite trading volume (limited only by gas costs). This can be used to manipulate external oracles, volume metrics, or reward systems that rely on the DEX's activity.

description.desc: No check preventing msg.sender from filling their own order

vulnerabilities.impact: The direct on-chain impact is distorted volume metrics via OrderPlaced and OrderFilled events. Any external protocol, oracle, dashboard, or reward system that reads these events or queries order fill data from the DEX will see inflated activity. While the attacker does not directly steal funds from the DEX itself through this mechanism, the missing self-trade check is a genuine code defect. In standard exchange design, self-trade prevention (STP) is a basic requirement. The severity is MEDIUM because the impact is primarily on data integrity rather than direct fund loss.

vulnerabilities.title: withdraw() Checks-Effects-Interactions Violation Enables Reentrancy Drain
vulnerabilities.summary: The withdraw() function calls IERC20(token).safeTransfer(msg.sender, amount) BEFORE updating balances[msg.sender][token] and totalDeposits[token]. If the token supports transfer callbacks (ERC777, ERC677, or tokens with hooks), an attacker can re-enter withdraw() during the transfer. Since the balance hasn't been decremented, the sufficiency check passes again, allowing repeated withdrawals draining all deposited tokens of that type.

description.desc: Line order: (1) safeTransfer to msg.sender, (2) balances -= amount, (3) totalDeposits -= amount. External call precedes state update—classic CEI violation

vulnerabilities.impact: If a callback-enabled token is used with this DEX, an attacker can drain all deposits of that token type. Combined with the emergencyWithdraw unchecked underflow bug (which can pre-inflate balances), the reentrancy becomes fully exploitable even with Solidity 0.8 checked arithmetic on unwinding. Impact: complete loss of all deposited callback-capable tokens.

vulnerabilities.title: UserBalance Struct Defined But Never Used — Dead Code Indicates Incomplete Implementation
vulnerabilities.summary: The UserBalance struct with 'available' and 'locked' fields is defined at lines 59-62 but never referenced anywhere in the contract. The actual balance tracking uses a simple uint128 mapping (balances[address][address]). This dead code strongly suggests the contract was designed to separately track available vs locked-in-orders balances but this separation was never implemented. As a result, the contract relies on implicit balance tracking (deducting from balances at placeOrder, adding back at c

description.desc: struct UserBalance { uint128 available; uint128 locked; } defined at lines 59-62 but never used; mapping(address => mapping(address => uint128)) public balances at line 73 uses flat uint128 instead

vulnerabilities.impact: Zero functional impact. The dead code wastes a small amount of bytecode but does not affect any runtime behavior. The hypothesis correctly notes this is a code quality issue about auditability, not a security vulnerability.
